briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon. We will now begin the climate briefing for July 3, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Deepening Global Climate Crisis and Extreme Phenomena
sections.content: Globally, the impacts of climate change are intensifying, leading to increased casualties and social disruption. Europe is experiencing debates over air conditioner use due to record-breaking heatwaves, and in France, over 2,000 excess deaths have occurred, even putting the world-renowned cycling event, the Tour de France, at risk of being shortened [17, 113]. The United States is also facing surging electricity demand, soaring electricity bills, and large-scale blackouts due to a heat dome phenomenon ahead of the Independence Day holiday, with researchers warning that such events would have been 'virtually impossible' without the climate crisis [111, 128]. Last month, the global sea surface temperature reached 20.86 degrees Celsius, breaking the record for June and amplifying concerns about heatwaves, heavy rainfall, and damage to marine ecosystems [112]. In addition, Iraq is experiencing new extreme temperatures, and reports continue to emerge that climate change is accelerating heavy rainfall and the growth of invasive species, indicating that the entire world is directly affected by the climate crisis [115, 124, 126].

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Efforts to Adapt to Climate Change and Strengthen National Systems
sections.content: The Republic of Korea is focusing on enhancing national climate crisis response capabilities based on climate change monitoring, prediction, and impact information. The Korea Meteorological Administration (KMA) is supporting these efforts by holding a forum on the 'Strategy for Strengthening the National Climate Crisis Response System' and promoting the establishment of an integrated climate information management system [1, 7, 8, 12]. In the agricultural sector, research is underway to address pests and diseases in subtropical crops in response to climate change. Cities like Gunsan are actively involved in developing and introducing new varieties, with Gunsan piloting the cultivation of a new 'triploid oyster' variety, and Seosan holding a regional adaptation evaluation for new potato varieties [2, 3, 4, 5, 9, 10, 15, 16]. Cheorwon County is exploring changes in crop cultivation methods by testing a 'Cheolgi50' rice double-cropping model, and Chonnam National University's College of Fisheries and Ocean Sciences is establishing a global research network to respond to the marine climate crisis, demonstrating ongoing adaptation efforts across various sectors [11, 13]. Furthermore, the Seoul Metropolitan Government is promoting a plan to strengthen its carbon-neutral urban agriculture policy, transforming urban agriculture into a central pillar of climate crisis response [14, 21, 44, 70]. The Climate Change Center is also collaborating with the law firm Barun to enhance its capacity to respond to climate regulations [6].

sections.title: 🌱 Accelerating Domestic Carbon Neutrality Implementation and Infrastructure Development
sections.content: Domestically, various policies and technological development efforts are actively underway to achieve carbon neutrality goals. Gwacheon City has secured national funding for the operation of its Carbon Neutrality Support Center, receiving 100 million KRW annually starting next year. Its proactive carbon neutrality policies have been recognized by the central government, including being uniquely selected as an additional support target nationwide [19, 26, 27, 28, 29, 32, 36, 38, 41, 43, 54, 64]. Hyundai Engineering & Construction is participating in a national project to develop a carbon dioxide (CO2) liquefaction, storage, and transport hub, securing Carbon Capture and Storage (CCS) infrastructure technology and strengthening its carbon neutrality competitiveness [23, 25, 39, 45, 46, 48, 50, 53, 57, 63, 65]. Korea South-East Power (KOEN) is sharing innovative high-efficiency CO2 capture and conversion technologies to strengthen carbon neutrality capabilities [35, 52, 56], and the Korea National Railway Authority is making efforts to accelerate the achievement of carbon neutrality goals by utilizing solar power infrastructure [31]. Additionally, Haenam County and Jangheung County are operating the 'Carbon Moa Tantani/e' project, which provides local currency as a reward for eco-friendly practices, encouraging citizens to engage in carbon-neutral activities. The Yeoncheon County Carbon Neutrality Support Center is fostering environmental sensitivity through educational programs for children [22, 34, 42, 47, 61, 62, 67].

sections.title: 🌱⚠️ Progress and Challenges in Renewable Energy Transition
sections.content: The expansion of renewable energy holds significant meaning not just as a climate policy but also as an energy security policy, with both ruling and opposition parties forming a consensus on this matter [73, 75, 90]. In particular, Cheong Wa Dae (the presidential office) announced plans to utilize a combination of nuclear and renewable energy to address concerns about power supply to the semiconductor cluster in the Honam region, emphasizing that renewable energy resources are abundant and have high potential for expansion [72, 76, 77, 80, 82, 84, 91, 92, 94, 102, 103, 106, 108]. In Hwaseong City, the 'No. 1 Sunlight Income Village' began operation as the first achievement of a resident-led renewable energy policy, demonstrating a case where solar power generation leads to welfare benefits [79, 97]. Korea Southern Power (KOSPO) is also operating a 'Renewable Energy 24-Hour Clinic' to provide one-stop support for renewable energy businesses [85, 87, 93]. However, numerous challenges remain, including power grid load issues in the era of renewable energy and AI, the sunset of the Renewable Portfolio Standard (RPS), and concerns about shrinking corporate investment due to changing renewable energy targets with each administration [88, 89, 104]. Ham Il-han, CEO of H-Energy, pointed out that the Korean renewable energy industry is at risk of falling into the hands of foreign capital and criticized the passive investment by domestic financial institutions [74].

sections.title: ⚠️ Domestic and International Challenges and Controversies Hindering Climate Action
sections.content: Despite efforts to address climate change, several domestic and international challenges and controversies are emerging. It has been pointed out that the greenhouse gas emissions from the domestic petrochemical industry increased by 1.2% last year, indicating no progress towards carbon neutrality [20]. Amidst intensifying competition to attract AI data centers, concerns have also been raised that this trend is difficult to reconcile with carbon neutrality goals [37]. An investigative report on Ganghwa County's preparedness regarding Article 34 of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act during its 9th popularly elected term has been published, indicating that local governments' climate response capabilities are also being scrutinized [69]. Internationally, a U.S. appeals court ruled that the Trump administration was not required to reinstall climate change-related signs and information it had removed from national parks, raising concerns about political interference in climate science [120, 129]. Furthermore, editorials are emerging, calling for the minimization of adverse effects that large-scale development projects, such as the 'Three Major Megaprojects,' might have on climate response. This highlights that finding a balance between economic growth and climate protection remains a difficult challenge [109].

briefing.closing: In today's briefing, we confirmed that various adaptation and carbon neutrality efforts are continuing both domestically and internationally, even amidst the deepening global climate crisis. However, at the same time, there are clearly challenges in the transition to renewable energy and difficulties hindering climate action. It is crucial to overcome the climate crisis through continuous attention and effort. This concludes today's climate briefing.
